topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The box was tucked away in the attic, smelling faintly of old paper and lavender. I hadn't meant to look, but something about the dust motes dancing in the single shaft of sunlight seemed to pull me toward it. Inside, stacked neatly, were notebooks from every grade, every subject. Calculus proofs, messy poetry, diagrams of the solar system—my mother’s meticulous archive of my education. A sharp, unpleasant knot tightened in my chest. I picked up a geometry textbook, the weight solid in my hands. "Seriously?" I muttered to the empty room, my voice tight. It felt like a betrayal, a monumental act of control I hadn't been privy to. I slammed the lid shut, the sound echoing unnervingly loud in the silence. I wouldn't touch anything else.

[story 2]

I found the collection hidden beneath a loose floorboard in the spare room. The sheer volume of completed work was overwhelming. Each spiral notebook was a testament to hours of focused effort, a silent record of my compliance. A slow, simmering anger began to bubble up, hot and prickly beneath my skin. I ran a hand over the spine of a history binder, the paper crisp beneath my fingertips. *She cataloged everything,* I thought, a bitter taste rising in my throat. I didn't want to feel grateful; I wanted to feel the frustration of having been managed, of having my every intellectual output filed away. I shoved the box back into its hiding spot with unnecessary force, my jaw clenched so tightly my teeth ached.

[story 3]

It was a surprise, this trove of forgotten homework. My mother had somehow preserved every test, every essay, every doodle. Standing there, surrounded by tangible evidence of my childhood compliance, I felt a sudden surge of resistance. I picked up a stack of English assignments, flipping through them quickly, my eyes scanning the neat handwriting. A scoff escaped my lips, sharp and dismissive. This wasn't a sentimental discovery; it was an inventory. I felt a distinct urge to disrupt the order, to scatter these carefully curated memories. With a decisive shove, I tipped the box over, letting the papers spill across the floor, a deliberate act of undoing.
